retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Genioglossus Muscle -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The ability to lower the tongue correctly allows better use of the vocal cords, for example, when using a wind instrument (clarinet).The color of the tongue requires evaluation. According to recent studies, for example, a yellow or bluish tongue may indicate the presence of type II diabetes mellitus.

retrieved_docs.title: Trauma to the Primary Dentition -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographs are an important diagnostic tool for diagnosing TDIs and serve as a baseline when compared to follow-up appointments. Intra-oral radiographs may include periapical radiographs (size 0 sensor/film, paralleling technique) or occlusal radiographs (size 2 sensor/film). Conventional radiology may not be possible in young children due to compliance, and a clinician may consider alternative views, such as lateral obliques. However, it is important to consider if taking an image and exposing the child to radiation will positively affect the diagnosis or treatment provided. Clinicians must work within the ALARA (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) principles to minimize radiation doses. The use of pulp testing in children is challenging; it is subjective and relies on the child’s understanding and cooperation. [19] False-positive/negative results can be misleading in clinical situations and therefore are not recommended in the IADT guidelines.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypoglossal Stimulation Device -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Standard equipment for the HGNS procedure includes the following: Head and neck surgical set Microdissectors Nerve stimulator Bipolar stimulation electrodes MDT tunneler MDT catheter passer Operating microscope Surgical loupes

retrieved_docs.title: Cleft Palate Repair -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Cleft palate repair sets should include the following: A fine suction cannula (e.g., Frazier 7 Fr) Mouth retractor (e.g., Dingman, Fisher, etc.) with tongue blades of different sizes. Periosteal elevators (e.g., Freer, Cronin, Molt #9, Warwick-James, Mitchel's trimmer, Barsky cleft palate rasp) Retractors (e.g., single hook, Guthrie hook) Long forceps (e.g., Gerald toothed, non-toothed forceps) Long scissors (e.g., Metzenbaum long scissors) Long needle holder (e.g., Rider) Electrosurgical knife (electrocautery) Diathermy with long Colorado tip Long bipolar electrocautery forceps Surgical loupes with 2.5-4x magnification are highly recommended for cleft palate repair; however, some surgeons may prefer to use an operating microscope.

retrieved_docs.title: Curriculum Design in Medical Simulation -- Procedural Skills Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Global Rating Scale (GRS): GRS is an assessment instrument that contains items that are considered relevant to each procedure, and every item has a scale to grade it. GRS is a commonly used tool to assess surgical procedures along with OSATS and can serve to assess the finesse or characteristics of a specific surgical action. For example, GRS can evaluate force used in a movement, respect for soft tissue, instrument handling, the fluidity of movement, etc., with a rating scale like the Linkert scale. Even though OSATS and GRS are different measurement tools, they are often used together to achieve a comprehensive assessment. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: CT Instrumentation and Physics -- Definition/Introduction -- Summary and additional points to consider
retrieved_docs.content: The application of mathematical filters called kernels serves to smoothen or sharpen data. Kernels serve to produce images with varying levels of noise and spatial resolution. A smooth kernel can help visualize soft tissue anatomy, while a sharp kernel has superior spatial resolution and is used to evaluate osseous structures. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Pit and Fissure Sealants -- Equipment -- Additional Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Additional equipment necessary for sealant placement typically includes: Air/water syringe Mouth mirror Explorer Excavator tip Squares of 2 x 2 gauze Cotton rolls Cotton pellets Forceps/cotton pliers Articulating paper Curing light Handpiece Dappen dish with pumice

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Bone Defect Morphology
retrieved_docs.content: Shallow vertical bone defects range from uneven crestal bone to defects of approximately 3 mm deep, easily recognized on radiographs. [11] A defect is considered deep if it is greater than 3 mm. Three-wall defects are typically found in posterior areas with wide interproximal bone consisting of a buccal, lingual, and interproximal wall. Three-wall defects may also be found at buccal or lingual furcations facing a bone shelf or exostosis. As the mesiodistal volume of the interproximal bone decreases, 3-wall defects may merge and form 2-wall defects called interproximal craters, where only the buccal and lingual walls remain. A 2-wall defect with a buccal and interproximal wall may develop adjacent to the entrance of the interproximal furcation of maxillary molars.

retrieved_docs.title: Anophthalmic Socket -- Evaluation -- Acquired Anophthalmic Socket
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical assessment of an acquired anophthalmic socket should focus on identifying any complications following enucleation or evisceration surgery. Certain ideal characteristics of the anophthalmic socket are also being evaluated (see Table 1. Ideal Anophthalmic Socket Characteristics).

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Closed Surgical Technique: This technique involves elevating, luxating, and delivering the root segment from the coronal aspect of the socket without creating a mucoperiosteal flap. Once appropriate surgical visualization of the root segment is achieved, dental elevators can be used in succession using one of three fundamental physics concepts: (1) a wheel, (2) a lever, and (3) a wedge. When using an elevator as a wheel, a rotational arc between the elevator tip and the body of the root develops to lift the root out coronally. Cryer elevators perform well when used in this manner. When using an elevator as a class 1 lever, a fulcrum point is created between the alveolar crest and the root. This concept requires a reliable purchase point so that force can be applied without instrument slippage or misdirection. [14] If the current purchase point is inadequate, it may be necessary to create a bony trough using a surgical handpiece or osteotome adjacent to the root. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2711
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic dilation of esophagus is linked to the procedural category upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy, indicating that it is considered an alternative or related mapping of that endoscopic examination. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy is classified under the same category of upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy, reflecting its role as a diagnostic approach that includes tissue sampling. Together, these statements illustrate that both the therapeutic Endoscopic dilation of esophagus and the diagnostic Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy belong to the broader domain of upper gastrointestinal endoscopic procedures, highlighting their complementary roles in evaluating and managing esophageal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 212
retrieved_docs.content: therapeutic actions comprise ربط دوالي المريء (esophageal variceal ligation) and مفاغرة مريئية (esophageal stenting). Diagnostic imaging such as upper gastrointestinal X‑ray is performed directly at the esophageal site (direct_procedure_site_of). This integrated network of procedural classifications, morphological descriptors, and disease entities supports clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for esophageal, variceal, and related upper‑gastrointestinal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2814
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic ultrasound of gastrointestinal tract is a diagnostic imaging procedure that is performed directly on the gastrointestinal system, enabling visualization of the organs and structures within the gastrointestinal tract for clinical assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2794
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal endoscopy (تنظير مريئي) is a diagnostic procedure that employs an internal endoscope (منظار داخلي) to visualize the esophageal lumen. This technique is categorized within the broader group of other gastrointestinal diagnostic procedures, indicating that it is one of several endoscopic methods used to assess the gastrointestinal tract. By using an internal endoscope, esophageal endoscopy provides direct inspection of the esophageal mucosa, contributing to the comprehensive evaluation of gastrointestinal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: Esophageal therapeutic procedures encompass a range of interventions that address structural and functional disorders of the esophagus. Correction of esophageal atresia and operations on esophageal varices are both classified as “عملية في المريء” (procedures in the esophagus). Within the broader category of “الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالمريء” (esophageal therapeutic procedures), esophageal dilatation is a key modality; it is also linked to the general concept of “عملية في المريء.” Esophageal dilatation includes both endoscopic dilation of the esophagus and endoscopic pneumatic dilation of the esophagus. These techniques are mapped from “upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy,” indicating that they are performed in the context of an upper GI endoscopic examination that may include tissue sampling. The procedure of upper gastrointestinal endoscopy with biopsy is itself classified under the same “upper gastrointestinal endoscopy; biopsy” descriptor, underscoring the close procedural
